speaker: interviewer
text: --with Kurt Saunders, August 16, 1996. Had you seen previous people who-- prior to liberating, getting these people out, had you seen anybody else from concentration camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I haven't seen any survivors.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was the first time. Then in what way did you-- did they impress as being different or strange?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were-- they were hard. They were tough. They were not nice. They didn't know-- even use a nice word. Please was unknown. Thank you was unknown to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long had they been in Theresienstadt?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have no idea.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they shared no experiences with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they didn't. It was just their general behavior. The-- well, they-- they behaved, maybe I could compare them, even though I had no contact with them, the street people maybe, the people asleep in the street here today. Maybe you could compare them to that.

speaker: interviewer
text: You could speak with them, because you speak German.

speaker: interviewee
text: They spoke German.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. And-- but-- and they spoke nothing to you. Did they complain about anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they-- they didn't even look into your eyes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- I mean, maybe it's the fear. In the meantime, I ask myself, how can I criticize these people or even think about it? Would I have been any different if I had survived there?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they briefed by other people or de-briefed? Did anybody question them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. We just brought them back. And that's where it stopped. I would run into them occasionally, because now they went to the German officers there, demanded money for food and so on. They asked them. And we turned them over. They became part of Mainz now. Their former citizens of Mainz. They lived there. They tried to regain some of their property if they had any property. As far as the Army, American Army, was concerned, we had done our job.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. You said you spent only about an hour there. There was a mine near Theresienstadt called Kladno. Were you familiar with the name, with any of the--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I knew nothing about Theresienstadt, just like the same story with Linz. We went to Linz to bring-- not survivors, bring back a Nazi.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: They had taken the fire engine from Mainz with art-- paintings and statues and crafts on it to Linz, because they have figured Hitler's going to make a stand there. Now, we were going there and bringing him back. So we went in there, told them a white lie because we're only in Jeep, that they should follow us. The people in Mainz want them back and so on, and nothing is going to happen to them. Naturally, when we got them back to Mainz, we arrested them. And we would stop and eat. And they wouldn't stop. They kept on going, that fire engine, they were so eager to get back to Mainz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they had [INAUDIBLE] on that. And-- well, so back to I didn't spend any time in Linz.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you spend in the Army in Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: From '43 to '46.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have any contact with anybody that you knew from your Austrian days or from your Belgium days, any Jews that--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: None at all.

speaker: interviewee
text: I wanted to go back to Austria. I arranged through my commanding officer-- I got a German Ford, got the car-- that I could go back to Austria. I made it as far as Munich, then the car broke down. I came back. And then I made a trip to France, to Reims. My brother had been wounded very badly and was supposed to go home. And then the breakthrough came in Belgium and he was sent back as a replacement for others that fought on the front, so I went to see my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you came back to America, you came back as an American citizen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you raised a family here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your experiences of being through some of the Nazi-- the Austrian Nazi and German Nazi [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] violence, discrimination, how did it impact on how you were raising your kids.

speaker: interviewee
text: I felt, being married to an American woman, that I raised them the American way. The only thing that I felt and always had a goal that my children, especially my daughters, should be able to stand on their own feet. Before we were talking about liberated women. I always felt that they have to stand on their own feet, because I always had the fear that what happened in Germany can happen here too. You asked me whether I got a job in England or in Belgium, and I said, no. But I met refugee girls. Well, a guy can get away without makeup and without stockings. A woman wants to have that makeup, wants to have the stockings. And many of the girls that I would have been proud to date in Vienna wound up in prostitution. And I felt if anything like this happens, my daughters should be prepared so they can stand on their own feet.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your son?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the son is different. Son was brought up too, but women should not be able-- should not depend on a man to survive. They should be able to survive themself. Naturally a son has to, same as I survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the Jewish life? How much of--

speaker: interviewee
text: All my kids had--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did it impact on their Jewish life?

speaker: interviewee
text: All my kids had Jewish education. They all went-- belonged to the temple. They all get religious education, every one of them. All of them married Jews. My son that didn't want to come here today said to me, for years I hated anything that was German. I hated any German, he said. Now, he says, I realize there are some good ones, he said. And I would have never married a Christian girl. He says, I married a Jewish girl. My daughters were a little more liberal. They all have-- all have a lot of Christian friends. But all married to Jews. My daughter, the one that's going to be here, the older one, the musician, she's even the godparent of two Catholic children. She has some very good friends. But they all married Jews. All had a Jewish education. They all know their Hebrew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do they know your story? Do they know how-- what happened to you in Austria?

speaker: interviewee
text: They know some of it. I have never-- I don't even describe here-- some of the things that I have seen in the streets I haven't described it. I just went over it.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're not stopping you.

speaker: interviewee
text: How they beat up-- how they beat up the people, how they smashed the windows, how they took children out in the park and beat them up. And it's-- when I talk to high school kids, I tell them one very simple thing. You can't talk, talk, and talk, but you cannot describe fear.

speaker: interviewer
text: You watched this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said-- I sometimes ask the kids, you ever had a nightmare and you wake up in the morning or you wake up in the middle of the night? And then you realize, that that was only a dream, I says. You're living a nightmare 24 hours a day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have nightmares?

speaker: interviewee
text: [CRYING] Too many died. In my family, too many died. Even my parents. They gave their life to the fatherland. My father had the Iron Cross, highly decorated. But even during the war, the First World War, my father collected money to plant trees in Palestine. I got the diploma that he had. We have always felt this way. And I always told my children, at least if anything happens here-- even though my daughter used to say, oh, you got a chip on your shoulder. It can't happen here. If anything happens here, at least you know you're a Jew. I said, I saw kids that were anti-Semitic in the school system in Vienna that turned out they discovered that they were Jews, half-Jews, a quarter Jews. And some of them-- I know of two that committed suicide. They couldn't take it. I could take it. I knew I was a Jew. I knew the Jewish history. I know it didn't start yesterday. It's been going on for centuries. My mother used to tell me about the programs in Poland, how they were hiding, how they murdered. It was the Cossacks. So this was nothing new. So now it's the German Cossacks. And if you write the history of Germany, Bismarck was an anti-Semite. Franz Joseph, yes, Franz Joseph was the guard, like Roosevelt was the guard in America. But his mother, Maria Theresa, tried to figure out how to kill all the Jews, because she inherited all those Polish Jews. And the Empress in Russia, she tried to figure out too how to kill the Jews, because when Germany and Austria and Russia split up Poland, each one got a part They didn't like the Jews. And we know. And OK, you could live with it. You survived-- we knew for thousands of years, the Jews have survived all these programs and everything else. They have always been survivors. And we're going to survive the Nazis too. Nobody expected that the Nazi's going to have the Final Solution.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the hardest part for you in your escape from-- from Austria through this whole thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: The hardest part was coming back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Coming back where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the American Army, landing on the beaches and get the smell of death. I don't know whether you ever been to Secaucus, where they had the slaughter houses there, that stench? That was the stench on the beaches, rotten corpses, rotten animals. That was the hardest part, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because at that time, I didn't have any idea. I knew about concentration camps. I had no idea about the gas chambers. We heard very little of that, even though our government knew about it already. We know now that the American government knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were escaping--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --what kept you going?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can-- I'll be honest with you. If I didn't have my brother with me, I most probably wouldn't be here today. My brother was an obligation. My mother, there was a very unique relationship. My brother was her little angel. He could never do anything wrong. I was the boy that always got into trouble, got into fights and everything else. My brother was the good boy. Even when he did something wrong, it couldn't be him. It had to be Kurt. And when I took-- when-- and I always felt that my mother in a way didn't trust me. But when she let me take that little angel of hers, called Angel, with me, she gave me her dearest. And I had to take care of him. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had to make sure that he comes out of it alive. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I think indirectly-- you know, every action has a reaction-- he saved my life. And coming to America and in-- 'til the end-- he died this year, on January 1. He contracted-- he was-- he had a dental lab, rather large dental lab, and he worked a lot with mercury. And naturally, years ago, we had no idea what mercury can do to you. And mercury got into his system. He got cancer of the bile duct and it spread to the liver, from the liver to the lung. I cannot tell you. I couldn't save him from that. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I could save him from everything, but not that. [CRYING] And my son today, there's one other excuse, but he doesn't want to come here. He lost his mother two and a half years ago, and now he lost his favorite uncle, my brother. [CRYING] My kids loved him. They loved Walter. I did too. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: Why--

speaker: interviewee
text: I lost my wife too in a car accident, a crash, and I loved her. This was worse than anything else. I could-- I could cope with Hitler. I can't cope with this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you choose to tell your story now, at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because it's necessary. I think that some of the money that's being spent in the United States opening up more Holocaust museums, I think some of the money is being wasted. That money should be spent opening up a clinic to study. We have clinics for cancer. We have clinic for AIDS. We have clinic for this and that, for Al disease. We need a clinic to study what makes a human animal an animal, because there's only other-- only one other animal that kills its own. Those are rats. They form armies and they kill an invader that enters their territory. No other animal will kill their own. They'll kill another animal for food. But human beings kill one another. We need to study it. That's how I feel. We have to find some way to stop this. It's not only the Jews. You look around, it hasn't stopped. It's the Muslims today killed by Christian and Christians by Muslims and Christians by Christians, the Protestants killing the Catholics, and vice versa. Go back in the history, the 30 Year Wars and Protestant burned Catholics at the stake alive, Catholic burnt the Protestants. There's killing. Only-- the only thing is Jews have been killed before. Only the Germans with their [GERMAN], their thoroughness, developed a system how they could exterminate people.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the basis is the same. You kill your own. You kill your own people. And I think this is what should be started, psychologist, doctors, find out, what is in the human brain? I mean, there hasn't been a day without war, without killing. Yeah. We have kind of come to the end of your testimony. We do have a number of pictures to show.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to thank you for sharing your testimony. And I'm going to cut the tape and show the pictures now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then when your daughter's arrive, we'll--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. You want me to describe the pictures, tell you what they're about?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. I will tell you what we do. Just remain seated and we will start on the pictures.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture taken during the First World War. It belonged to my dad. He's the one that's sitting on the chair right in the middle of this group of men. I don't know who the others are. This was taken in Russia. He spent 43 months in Russia during the First World War. He was seven years in the Army, Austrian Army. He was a first sergeant, called a [GERMAN].

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father's name, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Paul, but he was known as Herman.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his last name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Schatzker.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Out of my father's album. We had some of the stuff-- I don't remember how we got it out of Austria. But some of the pictures were sent out. As a matter of fact, I got a picture the other day sent by a cousin of mine, when we were small children. She sent it to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this picture in America? Did this picture come with you to America? Or did you find it in America?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were two suitcases we brought to the United States. They belonged to my parents. Naturally, they never made it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture that was taken in 1918 in Vienna. My father had just gotten engaged. He had a furlough, the first furlough from the front, the Russian front. The man with a beard on the right-hand side, where my father's elbow is, is his father, the rabbi. The woman sitting there is his stepmother. The lady on the left-hand side is his sister Sabine. The little boy standing up is that Uncle Alfred, the one that we [INAUDIBLE]. He became the editor, the Communist editor. He was the only boy among all these girls. There was one other sister of my father's that's not in the picture. Her name was Marie. The other lady that's standing up on the right-hand side behind her father is the lady that I was staying with for a while while I had a foster mother. She had-- well, she still has two surviving children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was Sabine. She has two surviving children. One is in France. He's a painter. And the other one is in Sweden. And it's a long story. I don't want to go into that.

speaker: interviewer
text: When do you think this-- oh, you know when this picture was taken, when your parents got engaged.

speaker: interviewee
text: The war was over November 11, and this was taken before. It was taken-- in September of 1918, they got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get this?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same as the other pictures.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know really whether this is an engagement picture or a wedding picture. But it was taken about the same time. It's a close-up and shows my mother my dad. And this is about it. It doesn't show anything else, except that my mother had beautiful legs that you can't see in this picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother's name, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Klara, [HEBREW] in Jewish, and she was a Goldstein. That was her maiden name. And that's about the story.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you get this picture in the same way as you get the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken before Hitler came. I don't know how many years before, but I know this is my father's father, grandfather. He was a rabbi in Vienna. He was also the prison chaplain at the [GERMAN].

speaker: interviewer
text: At the where?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the [GERMAN].

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, the [GERMAN]. And what does that mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the name of the court for the land. [INAUDIBLE] And he used to come home and tell us, Jews are crooks. Jews are thieves. Jews are everything. But no Jewish murderers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your grandfather's name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Schloime Schmiel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Klaar.

speaker: interviewer
text: Klaar.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [INAUDIBLE]. Klaar is a [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Part of the same pictures where my parents [INAUDIBLE]. I don't know exactly how we got these pictures.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my brother and I. I'm the guy on the right-hand side. We wore lederhosen and all the garb that a good Austrian wears in those days. I'm six years old. My brother is three years old. There's only a few days-- he was born on June 26 and I was born on June 11, three years apart. At his birth and later on, he had blond hair and his blue eyes. He always had blue eyes. I have black hair and brown eyes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your brother's name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother's name was Walter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember--

speaker: interviewee
text: [HEBREW]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember why this picture was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, every parent wants to show off their kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when it was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, if I was six years old, it was taken in 1925.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. I-- I meant do you remember--

speaker: interviewee
text: The occasion?

speaker: interviewer
text: The occasion.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't remember the occasion. All I know is that my mother was so proud of her two sons that everybody-- she said everybody in the family named their kids to Kurt and Walter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your brother's last name Saunders--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This picture was taken in Vienna in January of 1938. My cousin, one bride there, got married in January, just before Hitler came. And I'm the fourth guy on the left standing up. My brother, if you count from the other side-- one, two, three, four, five-- is the fifth one standing in the background there. We were at that wedding. It was wonderful, a big affair, because my uncle was wealthy. He was rich, in plain English.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's the bride's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Paulie. Paula.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Goldstein. She married a Wilka. And she is still alive. I just celebrated her 80th birthday in Jerusalem, as a matter of fact on that Saturday that Rabin was murdered.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the man she married, what's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: He is Wilka, but he's not alive. He's gone. And about-- I would about say 1/3 of the people in the picture are still alive. Others were killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: That picture I had with me, surprisingly. I don't know why I took it with me. I think I wanted to show off. There's another picture of me in the tuxedo. Maybe I wanted to show it off, the top hats and tuxedos.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This is my mother and father and a friend in Brussel. After my parents escaped to Brussel, this picture was taken in a park. And this young lady standing there was of great help.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is she standing? Which one is the [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the right-hand side. My mother's on the left. My father's in the middle. She is on right-hand side. She was the baker's daughter where we were hiding out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Marie. Yes. She was unique. She used to be in the chorus of the opera in Brussel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: I brought this out with me. I had that picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: You saw-- that's when you saw your parents in Belgium?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is this the last time you saw your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the passenger list of the My brother and I and a cousin of ours came on the And it-- we left, actually, it said February 10th. We arrived on February 22nd in New York. What's unique about that list is that a Max Frankel, who is the editor of the New York Times, was on that boat too. And I wrote to him. And I got an answer. He was nice enough to answer. And there are a few other names, people that I met later on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Was the trip in any way dangerous? Did you encounter any--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: The seas.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was pleasant. It was a pleasant trip.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture that was sent to me by my dad. After they fled Belgium, ahead of the German Army and to France, they got to southern France, the so-called Free France. He was interned at the camp called Gurs. Now, my father is the man-- if you count the first row, you count from the left three people there's a man right behind the third man. He doesn't wear a shirt and his arms are bare too. That's my father in that camp. And from Gurs, naturally, later on he and my mother were united and shipped to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father sent you this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. He sent this out.

speaker: interviewer
text: To where?

speaker: interviewee
text: To America, because for a while we were sending food packages to that camp in Gurs, and some money too. We could send out monthly food packages.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was this again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, after Hitler took Belgium. Let's see. '39-- must have been-- must have been '41 or '42, I think. I don't know the exact date. I could look it up.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's OK. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: It has-- in '42, they were deported to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this had to be before that.

speaker: interviewee
text: In August, '42. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, this is my wedding picture. I married this beautiful redhead, green-eyed girl. Her parents came from Russia. And I had the chutzpah to go out and buy an officer's uniform, so I wanted to look better. And I bought it. I didn't care about the stripes. It was the uniform. And I married this beautiful girl. Thank god I was married to her for over 50 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1943 in the Bronx. And we got married in a wedding hall there, but the picture was taken at the photographer's office.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is another wedding, 50 years later on August 15, exactly the same day, on a Sunday, that we got-- we were remarried by Rabbi Eichhorn of Temple Emanuel in Kingston, who is a friend of mine. And, well, we have changed a bit in the 50 years. I got white hair now. But my wife is still red with a little help. Her hair used to be much lighter when I first met her, but the smile is the same. The eyes are green. And well, things have changed, but it was wonderful. It was very enjoyable.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Here's my wife again. And my daughter on the right-hand side is Karen. Karen got the voice of her mother. My wife had a beautiful voice and my daughter is a musician and a singer. And Gayle on the left-hand side, in front of my wife, she is a goldsmith, internationally known, has lectured in university in Tokyo. My daughter is tonight performing at the Raleigh. She's going to be in Colorado Springs with the Philharmonics. And I could go on and go on and brag about my daughters.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what name does Karen use?

speaker: interviewee
text: Karen uses the name Saunders. And Gayle uses the name Saunders too. They're liberated women. They use their maiden names.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken a couple of years ago. This is my son Paul. And the boy next to him is Ryan. He has his arm on his-- I think he has his arm around his father. Then the little cocker there, I call him a cocker, is Jared. But he's smart, that kid. And then there's Shayna. She's the only girl in the-- my part of the family. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was that taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken at their home on Long Island.

speaker: interviewer
text: When?

speaker: interviewee
text: About two years ago. Jared now-- well, Ryan is 10 years old. He's going to be 11. Jared just was 5 years old and Shayna just passed seventh birthday.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of the house I was born in in Vienna, in the Second District, Schreygasse number 9. And make a long story short, nothing has changed there. This picture was taken by a cousin of mine that had gone back to Vienna. And it was taken after the Second World War, after the end of the war. And I was delivered in that building by a midwife. This picture was taken in High Falls, New York. My daughter Gayle made me a 70th birthday party. My brother Walter, who's on the left, is here. And in the background is a picture of my brother and I that you saw on the little cart that they blew up when we were little boys, 6 and 3. And this is to remind us how we have grown.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old is this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Seven years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Seven years.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was 70 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
